title_en: Monica Lewinsky Has Always Hated Notifications
title_zh: 莫妮卡·刘易斯基向来讨厌通知

paragraphs.en: If there’s one thing that’d convince someone to be mindful about their news consumption, it’s becoming the maligned subject of a political scandal at 24 years old. So when Google began offering news alerts in 2003, Monica Lewinsky made the deliberate decision not to partake. “In the first handful of years, when there was still legal stuff happening, I would panic if I was away from my phone and I had a lot of missed calls,” Lewinsky says. “I would think, Oh, I’ve been indicted, or something’s happened.’’ It’s not surprising, then, that Lewinsky has “never been a notifications person.” Now a contributing editor to Vanity Fair and the host of the podcast Reclaiming With Monica Lewinsky, she tells WIRED how her drafts folder helps with impulse control and why an iPad is key to surviving LA traffic.
paragraphs.zh: 如果说有什么事情会让人开始认真思考自己如何消费新闻，那就是在24岁时成为一场政治丑闻中的被指责对象。2003年，谷歌开始推出新闻提醒时，莫妮卡·刘易斯基便有意识地决定不去使用它。“在最初几年，当时还有很多法律纠纷要处理时，我一旦离开手机、错过了很多来电就会慌张，”刘易斯基说，“我会想，天哪，我是不是被起诉了，或者又发生了什么事。”因此，她“从来都不是什么‘通知控’”。如今，她是《名利场》的特约编辑，也是播客《与莫妮卡·刘易斯基一起重拾主权》的主持人，她在《连线》杂志上讲到，草稿文件夹如何帮助她控制冲动，以及为何iPad是她在洛杉矶堵车中生存的关键。

paragraphs.en: I have always been an iPhone girl, and I probably always will be an iPhone girl.
paragraphs.zh: 我一直是个 iPhone 女孩，而且我可能永远都是个 iPhone 女孩。

paragraphs.en: I also have an iPad Air. Anybody in LA knows if you have to cross the 405 in either direction, you don’t want to have to go back and forth during the day. So I probably mostly use my iPad when I know I’m going to be out for the day. I have a little iPad holder stand doohickey so that I can do my Zooms from the car.
paragraphs.zh: 我还有一台 iPad Air。洛杉矶的人都知道，无论要往哪边穿越405号公路，你都不想在一天中来回往返。所以我大多只在知道自己一整天都要在外时使用iPad。我有一个小小的iPad支架，让我可以在车里开视频会议。

paragraphs.en: A regular series that quantifies the tech lifestyles of the rich and/or famous.
paragraphs.zh: 这是一个常规栏目，量化富人和/或名人的科技生活方式。

paragraphs.en: I’m not someone who pays attention to those numbers. The only notifications I get are for text messages. I don’t do any for social media. I never accept a “Can we send you alerts?” I’d say 75 percent of the time I’m on my phone it’s because I need to be. If I’m spending too much time doomscrolling, or otherwise-scrolling, I usually notice it and try to be mindful of, like, “OK, am I trying to escape something?” I give myself permission to a lot. Especially these days. Very early on, when you could have Google notifications for news things, I very consciously chose not to do that. That relationship with technology and my nervous system started to become really clear to me before I even understood anything about being mindful of our nervous system.
paragraphs.zh: 我不是那种会在意这些数字的人。我的通知只有短信会发来。我对社交媒体不做任何提醒。我从不接受“我们可以给你发送提醒吗？”这种设置。大约有75%的时候我拿手机，是因为必须拿。要是我在刷到“末日式”信息，或者总之只是一直刷着时，我通常会意识到并提醒自己："好吧，我是不是在想逃避什么？"我会给自己很多允许。尤其是最近几个月。这是很早以前就有的：当时你还可以为新闻类内容开谷歌推送时，我就非常有意识地选择不这样做。实际上，我与科技和我自己神经系统的关系在我甚至还没真正理解“关注我们神经系统的状态”这件事之前，就已经变得很清晰了。

paragraphs.en: I’d say in the last year and a half, most of the podcasts I’m listening to are part of the research I do for my own [podcast] interviews. I like to listen to people before I have a conversation with them. I often do that, or listen to a book that someone’s written, on double speed—that’s where I think my ADHD comes in handy: “Oh, I can process really quickly.” I also tend to take a lot of my calls in the car in LA, or if I’m walking when I’m in New York. I try to walk everywhere. I tend to do calls rather than having downtime to listen to music.
paragraphs.zh: 我想在过去一年半里，我听的大多数播客，都是我为自己的【播客】采访做准备时做的研究。我喜欢在和某人对谈前先听听他们。通常我就是这样做，或者以两倍速听某人写的书——这正是我觉得自己的注意力缺陷多动障碍派上用场的地方："哦，我能处理得很快。"我也常常在洛杉矶开车时接很多电话，或者在纽约时边走路边接电话。我尽量步行到处走。与其空出时间听音乐，不如我更倾向于通电话。

paragraphs.en: I think it was at 40,000 at some point. I wish I were an inbox-zero person. I would love to be that.
paragraphs.zh: 我想我在某个时候达到了4万。我要是能成为那种“收件箱清零”的人就好了。我真希望自己是那样的人。

paragraphs.en: They’re 7 and 10. When I lived in New York and they were in LA, I would read to them over FaceTime very often. I would get two copies of every book so they could follow along.
paragraphs.zh: 他们今年7岁和10岁。以前我住在纽约、他们在洛杉矶的时候，我会经常通过FaceTime给他们读书。我会买两本同样的书，让他们也能一起跟着看。

paragraphs.en: I was at a bar mitzvah. One of the young people from the Amanda Knox show that I executive produced was there with her new girlfriend, and I stole a picture. They were holding hands, and I thought it was cute.
paragraphs.zh: 那天我在一场割礼。阿曼达·诺克斯节目组里的一位年轻人也在场，她带着新女友过来，我偷偷拍了一张。她们手牵着手，我觉得很可爱。

paragraphs.en: Their algorithm is good, for what it serves up—even the ads. The visual conversations that happen on Instagram are more interesting than the kinds of conversations that are happening now on Twitter. I use Twitter on occasion, but the algorithm changed in a way that my experience on there is a lot more unpleasant, with the kinds of comments I see or where my posts get exposed. And I like Threads, but visually it doesn’t work for me. They could use a redesign. What’s nice about Threads is that it feels like a much more cohesive community. You just feel sort of safe to open any conversation there. It might be echo chamber-y, but at least you don’t worry about tech assaults.
paragraphs.zh: 它的推荐算法确实不错，展示出来的内容都很合适，连广告也是如此。Instagram 上发生的视觉化交流，比现在在 Twitter 上发生的交流更有意思。我偶尔会用 Twitter，但算法变了之后，我在那里的体验变得更不舒服了——无论是看到的评论，还是我的帖子被曝光的方式。而且我喜欢 Threads，不过在视觉体验上并不适合我，它们确实需要重新设计。Threads 的优点是让我觉得这里像一个更有凝聚力的社区。你在那儿发起任何话题都会有种更安全的感觉。它或许有点回音室效应，但至少你不用担心遭遇网络攻击。

paragraphs.en: I’m not an early adopter of anything. I have a friend who’s an IP attorney, and I remember having dinner with him in 2005. I was leaving for graduate school and I was like, “Oh, what are you working on?” And he said, “Oh, protecting videos and things on phones.” And I’m like, “Who would watch anything on a phone? What an idiot!”
paragraphs.zh: 我从来都不是任何东西的早期采用者。我有一个朋友是知识产权律师，我记得 2005 年和他吃晚饭时的情景。我当时正要去读研究生，我问他：“你现在在做什么呢？”他说：“啊，我在做手机里的视频和相关内容的保护。”我当时就想：‘谁会在手机上看这些东西？真是个傻瓜！’

paragraphs.en: I don’t go to a specific outlet every day for news. I sort of rely on the curation, whether I’m getting my news from The New York Times or Vanity Fair or The Atlantic.
paragraphs.zh: 我每天不会固定去某一个媒体平台获取新闻。我更依赖内容精选，不管是看《纽约时报》，还是《名利场》或《大西洋月刊》。

paragraphs.en: Every time I hear somebody from the Reddit community speak somewhere about Reddit, I think, “Oh, I should be using it more.” But then also there’s not enough time in the day.
paragraphs.zh: 每次听到有人在某个地方谈论 Reddit，我都会想，‘哦，我应该更多地去用用它。’但又总会想到，时间真的不够用。

paragraphs.en: It connects to my iPad for use with my therapist on Zoom.
paragraphs.zh: 它可以连到我的 iPad，方便我在 Zoom 上和我的治疗师一起使用。

paragraphs.en: Number of burner accounts: 2
paragraphs.zh: 小号数量：2 个

paragraphs.en: Technology you’re most nostalgic for: old Google algorithm
paragraphs.zh: 你最怀念的技术：旧版 Google 算法

paragraphs.en: I miss the old Google. The ability to search was a lot easier.
paragraphs.zh: 我怀念旧版 Google。搜索起来曾经容易得多。

paragraphs.en: Where you fall on the tech hygiene spectrum:
paragraphs.zh: 你在技术卫生意识上处于什么位置：

paragraphs.en: I am most mindful of trying to protect my mental health and being sensitive to a problem with ADHD of, “Everybody’s mad at me.” I wish that there were easier ways than just a wink or something else for someone to be like, “I’m in sarcasm mode.” I joke about this a lot, but my drafts folder—I try to be really mindful of what I put out into the world. There are times when I really want to respond in a snarky way back to someone, and I have to try a little to practice what I preach and be mindful about what we put on the internet. I think that’s the thing that we tend to forget so easily with the distance of a screen: We’re in connection and in conversation with people through technological devices.
paragraphs.zh: 我最在意的是尽量保护自己的心理健康，并对ADHD中的一个问题保持敏感，即“每个人都在对我生气”。我真希望人们有比眨眼或其他动作更直观的方法，来表达“我现在是讽刺模式”。我常开玩笑说这些，但我在草稿文件夹里也会很在意自己要发布到外界的内容。有时我真的很想回一句挖苦的话，但我必须努力实践自己讲过的原则，留意我们在网上发布的内容。我觉得这就是我们在屏幕隔着距离时最容易忘记的一点：我们其实是在通过技术设备与人建立联系并进行对话。

paragraphs.en: A version of this article will appear in the June/July issue. Subscribe now.
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章的一个版本将于6月/7月号发布。立即订阅。
